title: Linear poroelastic response of thin permeable gel films
authors: Caroline Kopecz-Muller, Joshua D Mcgraw, Thomas Salez
abstract: When a hydrophilic and deformable porous material is immersed in a bath, it may absorb the solvent and expand by several times its volume, thus forming a highly soft and porous hydrogel. A stress applied on the soft hydrogel surface deforms it and forces the absorbed solvent to move by flowing through the network of pores. This coupled phenomenon sets the framework of poroelasticity. Moreover, polymeric gels are often used in ultra-thin coatings to tune surface properties. Together with the characteristic poroelastic coupling, this thinness challenges the modelling of their response. In this article, we derive the point-force mechanical response of a thin, permeable and poroelastic layer bounded to a rigid substrate. We show that the gel surface is only deformed around the indentation point, within a radius on the order of the layer thickness. The obtained Green's function can be directly used to predict the space- and time-dependent surface deformation of the gel. Our findings are relevant for a broad range of applications, such as indentation experiments on swollen gels, thin membranes or soft and living systems, as well as lubrication problems involving a soft and porous wall, for instance in microfluidics.
summary: This paper develops a mathematical framework to model how thin, porous gel coatings deform when a force is applied. It specifically addresses the challenge of finite thickness, showing that the mechanical response is confined to a region near the contact point. The researchers derived a Green's function that allows for the prediction of how these coatings relax from an incompressible to a compressible state. These findings are highly applicable to microfluidics, soft robotics, and biological membrane studies.
main_problem: Deriving the linear poroelastic Green's function to predict the space- and time-dependent mechanical response of a thin, permeable gel layer bounded to a rigid substrate.
main_result: The study provides an analytical expression for the poroelastic response, showing that deformation is localized to a radius comparable to the layer thickness and transitions from an incompressible to a compressible elastic state over time.
method: The authors use an analytical approach involving Hankel and Laplace transforms in reciprocal space to solve the coupled poroelasticity equations and numerical inversion (Talbot algorithm) for real-space profiles.
model_or_system: A thin, hydrophilic, and permeable poroelastic gel layer of thickness tau, bounded at the bottom by a rigid, impermeable substrate and exposed to a solvent bath at the top.
key_observables: Surface normal deformation profile w(r, t) and the poroelastic Green's function G_tau(r, t).
important_parameters: Layer thickness (tau), shear modulus (G), permeability (k), Poisson ratio (nu), and solvent viscosity (eta).
main_assumptions: Linear poroelasticity (small deformations), isotropic material, incompressible solvent, and a rigid/impermeable substrate.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of the system and applied force; Figure 2 displays deformation in reciprocal space across different temporal frequencies; Figure 3 shows scaled deformation vs. spatial frequency; Figure 4 shows real-space deformation profiles at different times/thicknesses; Figure 5 illustrates the relaxation of a gate-shaped pressure field.
paper_structure: The paper begins with a system definition and governing poroelastic equations, moves to a mathematical derivation using potential functions and transforms in reciprocal space, presents asymptotic analyses of various physical limits, and concludes with numerical results and applications to axisymmetric pressure fields.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Schematic of the system. A point force F0δ(r) is applied at the surface of a permeable poroelastic medium, of shear modulus G and Poisson ratio ν. A fluid of viscosity η is flowing through the pores of the gel, whose permeability is denoted by k. The resulting vertical deformation profile represented by the thick black line is denoted by w(r, t). We compute the poroelastic response function Gτ(r, t) of the material.

FIG. 2: Deformation profile of a finite-size poroelastic medium in reciprocal space triggered by a point force. A: Deformation ˆwτ(s, t) scaled by (F0/G)5/2/(πDpe), as a function of the dimensionless spatial frequency s p

FIG. 3: Deformation profile of a finite-size poroelastic medium, for various thicknesses of the layer. A: Deformation ˆwτ(s, t) scaled by (F0/G)5/2/(πDpe), as a function of the dimensionless spatial frequency s p
FIG. 4: Deformation profile of a poroelastic medium in real space. A: Deformation wτ(s, t) scaled by p
FIG. 5: Example: relaxation of the deformation profile generated by a gate-shaped pressure field. Deformation wτ(r, t) scaled by p
